note.soap:
S:  
Patient is a 13-17-year-old from Georgia presenting with a tick bite and fever with chills for 24-72 hours. No rash reported. Pregnancy not possible.

O:  
No physical exam available due to phone-only encounter. No rash observed or reported. Fever and chills present.

A:  
- Working diagnosis (most likely): Early tick-borne illness (possible early Lyme disease or other tick-borne infection) given tick exposure and fever without rash.  
- Differential:  
  1. Early Lyme disease (though no rash reported)  
  2. Other tick-borne illnesses (e.g., ehrlichiosis, anaplasmosis)  
  3. Viral infection unrelated to tick bite  
  4. Bacterial infection unrelated to tick bite  
- Red flags considered: Persistent high fever, altered mental status, severe headache, neck stiffness, difficulty breathing, or signs of sepsis—all denied or not reported.

P:  
- Disposition: Urgent in-person evaluation recommended due to fever and tick bite in an endemic area to allow physical exam, diagnostic testing, and possible empiric treatment.  
- Plan: Advise patient/guardian to seek urgent care or primary care visit within 24 hours for clinical evaluation, blood tests (CBC, blood smear, Lyme serology if indicated), and possible empiric antibiotics. Educate on signs of worsening illness.  
- Safety net: Instruct to seek emergency care immediately if symptoms worsen—high fever >39°C (102.2°F), severe headache, neck stiffness, confusion, difficulty breathing, or rash develops.

Evidence (snippets used):  
No direct evidence snippets retrieved; recommendations based on standard clinical practice for tick-borne illness evaluation and management in endemic areas.
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.

evidence.title: CDC: What to Do After a Tick Bite
CDC: Guidance for Clinicians—Caring for Patients after a Tick Bite (PDF)
IDSA/AAN/ACR: 2020 Guidelines for Prevention, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Lyme Disease
AAFP: Lyme Disease—Updated Recommendations from IDSA/AAN/ACR (2021)
